## Cross-Entropy Loss

Cross-entropy measures how wrong a predicted probability distribution is.
It gives training a single number to minimize, so better predictions become lower loss.
For next-token prediction, it becomes the model's training loss: the model is punished when it assigns low probability to the actual next token.

### Cross-Entropy

Given a true probability distribution $p$ and the model's predicted distribution $q$, cross-entropy is:

$$
H(p, q) = -\sum_i p_i \ln q_i
$$

In next-token prediction, $p$ is one-hot: the correct token has probability $1.0$, and every other token has probability $0.0$.
That makes the sum collapse to one term.
If the correct token is $c$, the loss is:

$$
H = -\ln q_c
$$

The implementation receives raw logits, turns them into probabilities with softmax, and returns the negative log probability of the correct token.

<!-- snippet: src/python/train.py#cross_entropy_loss -->
```python
def cross_entropy_loss(logits: Sequence[float], true_id: int) -> float:
    probabilities = softmax(logits)
    return -math.log(max(probabilities[true_id], 1.0e-12))
```
<!-- /snippet -->

Suppose the vocabulary has four tokens and the model assigns probabilities $[0.1, 0.6, 0.2, 0.1]$.
If the correct token index is $1$, then the probability of the correct answer is $0.6$.
The loss is:

$$
H = -\ln(0.6) \approx 0.511
$$

If the model assigned probability $1.0$ to the correct token, the loss would be $0.0$.
If it assigned $0.01$, the loss would jump to $4.605$.
Low confidence in the correct answer is expensive, as @fig-ch02-cross-entropy shows.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch02-cross-entropy
#| fig-cap: "Cross-entropy loss grows steeply as the model assigns less probability to the correct token"
#| echo: false

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

DARK='#222222'; BLUE='#1177BB'; RED='#CC2222'; MONO='monospace'

q = np.linspace(0.01, 1.0, 300)
H = -np.log(q)
perp = np.exp(H)

fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4), facecolor='white')

ax1.set_facecolor('white')
ax1.plot(q, H, color=BLUE, lw=2.5)
ax1.axvline(0.6, color=RED, lw=1.2, linestyle='--', alpha=0.7)
ax1.axhline(-np.log(0.6), color=RED, lw=1.2, linestyle='--', alpha=0.7)
ax1.plot(0.6, -np.log(0.6), 'o', color=RED, markersize=7)
ax1.text(0.62, -np.log(0.6)+0.1, f'p=0.6 → H≈{-np.log(0.6):.3f}',
         fontfamily=MONO, fontsize=9, color=RED)
ax1.set_xlabel('probability of correct token', fontfamily=MONO, fontsize=11)
ax1.set_ylabel('cross-entropy loss H = -ln(q)', fontfamily=MONO, fontsize=11)
for sp in ['top','right']: ax1.spines[sp].set_visible(False)

ax2.set_facecolor('white')
ax2.plot(q, perp, color=BLUE, lw=2.5)
ax2.set_ylim(0, 30)
ax2.set_xlabel('probability of correct token', fontfamily=MONO, fontsize=11)
ax2.set_ylabel('perplexity = exp(H)', fontfamily=MONO, fontsize=11)
for sp in ['top','right']: ax2.spines[sp].set_visible(False)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### Perplexity

Perplexity converts cross-entropy back into an easier-to-read scale:

$$
\text{perplexity} = e^H
$$

The implementation is one line because this is exactly the natural exponential from Section 2.5.

<!-- snippet: src/python/train.py#perplexity -->
```python
def perplexity(loss: float) -> float:
    return math.exp(loss)
```
<!-- /snippet -->

If $H = \ln(10.0)$, then $e^H = 10.0$.
A language model with perplexity 10 is as uncertain as choosing uniformly among 10 tokens.
